source_concept.label: Define analytics

relationships.correct_description: Analytics provides the data foundation that enables accurate forecasting
relationships.explanation: Analytics serves as a prerequisite for effective forecasting because the statistical methods, data processing techniques, and machine learning algorithms that comprise analytics are the very tools used to build forecasting models. Without robust analytical capabilities to process historical sales data, market signals, and trend patterns, demand forecasts would lack the quantitative rigor needed for reliable predictions. This connection reflects how data-driven decision-making underpins the entire demand planning function in supply chains.

sources.title: What Is Supply Chain Analytics?
What is supply chain analytics and why is it important?
What is Supply Chain Analytics: Definition, Types & Benefits

source_concept.label: Define analytics

relationships.correct_description: Supply market intelligence relies on analytics to gather and interpret market data
relationships.explanation: Supply market intelligence depends on analytics because the process of gathering, analyzing, and interpreting supplier market data requires sophisticated analytical tools and techniques. Analytics provides the computational power to process large volumes of pricing data, supplier performance metrics, and market trend information that feeds into supply market intelligence. This relationship highlights how analytical capabilities transform raw market data into actionable procurement intelligence that supports strategic sourcing decisions.

sources.title: What Is Supply Chain Analytics?
What is supply chain analytics and why is it important?
What is Supply Chain Analytics: Definition, Types & Benefits

source_concept.label: Define analytics

target_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Analytics is applied to optimize inventory management decisions
relationships.explanation: Analytics applies directly to inventory management by enabling data-driven decisions about safety stock levels, reorder points, and economic order quantities. Through descriptive analytics, organizations can understand historical inventory patterns, while predictive analytics helps anticipate demand fluctuations that affect stock requirements. This connection underscores how modern inventory management has moved beyond simple rule-based approaches to leverage sophisticated analytical methods for optimizing the balance between carrying costs and service levels.

sources.title: What Is Supply Chain Analytics?
What is supply chain analytics and why is it important?
What is Supply Chain Analytics: Definition, Types & Benefits

source_concept.label: Explain customer relationship management

relationships.correct_description: CRM data on customer buying patterns feeds into demand forecasting
relationships.explanation: CRM systems capture detailed customer purchasing histories, preferences, and behavioral patterns that serve as valuable inputs to demand forecasting models. By analyzing CRM data on buying frequencies, seasonal preferences, and customer segmentation, forecasting teams can produce more granular and accurate demand predictions. This relationship demonstrates how customer-facing data systems enhance supply chain planning by providing demand signals directly from the point of customer interaction.

sources.title: Customer relationship management
CRM in Logistics and Supply Chain Management
Customer Relationship Management and Supply Chain Management

source_concept.label: Explain customer relationship management

relationships.correct_description: CRM demand signals inform sales and operations planning
relationships.explanation: CRM systems generate demand signals through customer interactions, sales pipeline data, and market feedback that directly feed into the demand planning step of S&OP. The customer insights captured in CRM, such as upcoming orders, shifting preferences, and customer growth trajectories, help S&OP teams develop more accurate demand plans. This connection reflects how customer-facing intelligence must be integrated with operational planning to ensure supply chain capacity aligns with actual market demand.

sources.title: Customer relationship management
CRM in Logistics and Supply Chain Management
Customer Relationship Management and Supply Chain Management

source_concept.label: Explain customer relationship management

relationships.correct_description: Analytics capabilities enhance CRM insights and customer segmentation
relationships.explanation: Analytics enhances CRM by enabling deeper analysis of customer data, more precise segmentation, and identification of patterns that would be invisible through manual review. Advanced analytical techniques such as predictive modeling can identify at-risk customers, forecast customer lifetime value, and optimize marketing efforts within CRM systems. This relationship illustrates how the application of analytical methods transforms CRM from a simple data repository into a strategic tool for supply chain demand alignment.

sources.title: Customer relationship management
CRM in Logistics and Supply Chain Management
Customer Relationship Management and Supply Chain Management

source_concept.label: Describe the role of forecasting

relationships.correct_description: Analytics tools and techniques power forecasting models
relationships.explanation: Forecasting relies on analytics tools and techniques because the quantitative methods used in demand prediction, including time series analysis, regression modeling, and machine learning, are fundamentally analytical processes. Analytics provides both the computational infrastructure and the methodological framework that makes modern forecasting possible. This bidirectional connection with B.1->B.3 shows how analytics and forecasting are deeply intertwined, with analytics enabling forecasting and forecasting being one of the primary applications of supply chain analytics.

sources.title: How to do Demand Forecasting in the Supply Chain?
A Guide to Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain Management
What is Demand Forecasting?

source_concept.label: Describe the role of forecasting

target_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Forecasting determines optimal inventory levels and replenishment schedules
relationships.explanation: Forecasting is a prerequisite for effective inventory management because the determination of safety stock levels, reorder points, and order quantities all depend on predicted future demand. Without accurate demand forecasts, inventory planners cannot set appropriate stock levels, leading to either costly excess inventory or damaging stockouts. This fundamental dependency means that improvements in forecasting accuracy translate directly into better inventory performance, reduced carrying costs, and higher customer service levels.

sources.title: How to do Demand Forecasting in the Supply Chain?
A Guide to Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain Management
What is Demand Forecasting?

source_concept.label: Describe the role of forecasting

relationships.correct_description: Forecasting is a key input to the demand planning phase of S&OP
relationships.explanation: Forecasting is an integral component of S&OP because the demand planning step, which is one of the five main stages of the S&OP process, fundamentally relies on demand forecasts to set sales expectations. These forecasts drive the entire S&OP cascade, determining production plans, capacity requirements, and inventory targets. Without reliable forecasts feeding into S&OP, the cross-functional alignment between sales, operations, and finance that S&OP aims to achieve would lack a factual basis for planning.

sources.title: How to do Demand Forecasting in the Supply Chain?
A Guide to Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain Management
What is Demand Forecasting?

source_concept.label: Explain the growth of global distribution

target_concept.label: Explain the relationship between supply chain management and transportation planning

relationships.correct_description: Global distribution depends heavily on transportation planning across international routes
relationships.explanation: Global distribution networks require sophisticated transportation planning because moving goods across international borders involves coordinating multiple transportation modes, navigating customs requirements, and optimizing routes that span continents. The complexity of international freight management, including ocean shipping, air cargo, and cross-border trucking, demands careful carrier selection and route optimization. As global distribution networks expand, transportation planning becomes increasingly critical to managing longer lead times, higher costs, and the logistical challenges of multi-modal international shipments.

sources.title: Global supply chain management
Global Supply-Chain Management
Global Supply Chain Management

source_concept.label: Explain the growth of global distribution

target_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Global distribution requires sophisticated inventory management across multiple locations
relationships.explanation: Global distribution networks create inventory management challenges that are far more complex than domestic operations, requiring coordination of stock across warehouses, distribution centers, and fulfillment facilities in multiple countries. Organizations must determine optimal inventory positioning, manage varying lead times from international suppliers, and account for customs delays and currency fluctuations in their inventory cost calculations. The geographic dispersion inherent in global distribution amplifies the importance of accurate safety stock calculations and strategic stock placement to maintain service levels across diverse markets.

sources.title: Global supply chain management
Global Supply-Chain Management
Global Supply Chain Management

source_concept.label: Explain the growth of global distribution

target_concept.label: Define sustainability for supply chain management

relationships.correct_description: Global distribution has significant sustainability implications including carbon emissions
relationships.explanation: The expansion of global distribution networks has increased the environmental footprint of supply chains through higher carbon emissions from long-distance transportation, increased packaging waste, and greater energy consumption in international logistics. As goods travel longer distances across multiple transportation modes, the cumulative environmental impact grows substantially. This tension between global reach and environmental responsibility has made sustainability a critical consideration in global distribution strategy, pushing companies toward green logistics initiatives, carbon offset programs, and nearshoring strategies that reduce environmental impact.

sources.title: Global supply chain management
Global Supply-Chain Management
Global Supply Chain Management

source_concept.label: Explain supply chain management for healthcare industry

target_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Healthcare SCM requires specialized inventory management for medical supplies and pharmaceuticals
relationships.explanation: Healthcare supply chain management includes inventory management as a critical function because medical supplies and pharmaceuticals have unique requirements such as expiration date tracking, cold-chain temperature control, lot traceability, and regulatory compliance. Unlike general inventory management, healthcare inventory must prioritize product availability for patient safety above cost optimization, while still managing the rising costs of medical supplies. The specialized nature of healthcare inventory, including controlled substances, sterile products, and life-saving equipment, demands more rigorous inventory control procedures than typical commercial supply chains.

sources.title: Healthcare Supply Chain Management: Importance & Practices
Supply Chain Issues in Healthcare and How to Manage Shortages
Healthcare logistics and supply chain - issues and future challenges

source_concept.label: Explain supply chain management for healthcare industry

relationships.correct_description: Procurement of medical devices and pharmaceuticals is a core healthcare SCM function
relationships.explanation: Healthcare supply chain management includes procurement as a core function because the acquisition of medical devices, pharmaceuticals, and clinical supplies requires specialized sourcing processes that account for regulatory approvals, quality certifications, and clinical efficacy requirements. Healthcare procurement must navigate complex supplier relationships with medical device manufacturers and pharmaceutical companies, manage group purchasing organization contracts, and ensure compliance with FDA and other regulatory standards. The critical nature of these products for patient care elevates procurement decisions beyond cost considerations to include supply reliability, product quality, and clinical outcomes.

sources.title: Healthcare Supply Chain Management: Importance & Practices
Supply Chain Issues in Healthcare and How to Manage Shortages
Healthcare logistics and supply chain - issues and future challenges

source_concept.label: Explain supply chain management for healthcare industry

relationships.correct_description: Demand forecasting in healthcare must account for unpredictable patient needs and emergencies
relationships.explanation: Healthcare supply chain management has a particularly challenging relationship with forecasting because patient demand is inherently unpredictable compared to commercial markets. Emergency surges, disease outbreaks, and seasonal illness patterns create demand volatility that standard forecasting models may not adequately capture. Healthcare organizations must combine traditional statistical forecasting with epidemiological data, hospital admission trends, and emergency preparedness scenarios to maintain adequate supply levels while avoiding costly waste from expired products.

sources.title: Healthcare Supply Chain Management: Importance & Practices
Supply Chain Issues in Healthcare and How to Manage Shortages
Healthcare logistics and supply chain - issues and future challenges

source_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Forecasting drives inventory replenishment decisions and safety stock calculations
relationships.explanation: Inventory management relies on forecasting because every key inventory parameter, from safety stock levels to reorder points to economic order quantities, is calculated based on expected future demand. The accuracy of demand forecasts directly determines whether inventory levels are appropriately calibrated, making forecasting the primary informational input to inventory planning. This dependency means that improvements in forecast accuracy yield immediate benefits in inventory efficiency, reducing both excess stock costs and stockout risks.

sources.title: What Is Inventory Management?
Inventory Management - Definition, Importance, Parameters, Process & Example

source_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Procurement activities trigger inventory inflows based on inventory management policies
relationships.explanation: Inventory management and procurement are closely linked because inventory policies such as reorder points and minimum stock levels trigger procurement actions to replenish stock. The inventory management system determines when and how much to order, while procurement executes the actual purchasing process with suppliers. This interface between inventory planning and procurement execution is critical for maintaining continuous supply, as procurement lead times must be factored into inventory calculations and reorder timing.

sources.title: What Is Inventory Management?
Inventory Management - Definition, Importance, Parameters, Process & Example

source_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Inventory planning is a component of the supply planning phase in S&OP
relationships.explanation: Inventory management is a component of S&OP because the supply planning phase of the S&OP process must determine inventory targets that balance customer service requirements with cost constraints. During S&OP, inventory plans are developed that specify how much finished goods, work-in-progress, and raw materials should be held across the planning horizon. This integration ensures that inventory decisions are aligned with both the demand plan and production capacity, rather than being made in isolation by individual functional areas.

sources.title: What Is Inventory Management?
Inventory Management - Definition, Importance, Parameters, Process & Example

source_concept.label: Define procurement

relationships.correct_description: Supply market intelligence informs procurement decisions with market data and supplier analysis
relationships.explanation: Procurement depends on supply market intelligence because effective sourcing and supplier selection require detailed knowledge of market conditions, supplier capabilities, and pricing dynamics. Market intelligence provides procurement professionals with the information needed to negotiate favorable terms, identify alternative suppliers, and anticipate supply disruptions. Without this intelligence, procurement operates reactively rather than strategically, potentially missing opportunities for cost savings or failing to mitigate supply risks before they materialize.

sources.title: Supply Management Procurement
Procurement vs Supply Chain Management: What's the Difference?
SCM Definitions and Glossary of Terms

source_concept.label: Define procurement

target_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Procurement triggers inventory replenishment and manages supplier lead times
relationships.explanation: Procurement directly affects inventory management because the timing, quantity, and reliability of procurement activities determine when new stock arrives and at what cost. Procurement manages supplier relationships and lead times that are critical inputs to inventory planning calculations. When procurement secures shorter lead times or more reliable delivery schedules, inventory managers can reduce safety stock levels and improve working capital efficiency. This relationship highlights how procurement execution quality directly impacts inventory performance.

sources.title: Supply Management Procurement
Procurement vs Supply Chain Management: What's the Difference?
SCM Definitions and Glossary of Terms

source_concept.label: Define procurement

target_concept.label: Define sustainability for supply chain management

relationships.correct_description: Sustainable procurement practices support supply chain sustainability goals
relationships.explanation: Procurement plays a key role in supply chain sustainability because the selection of suppliers and the terms of purchasing agreements directly influence environmental and social outcomes across the supply chain. Sustainable procurement evaluates suppliers not only on cost and quality but also on environmental practices, labor standards, and ethical sourcing. By incorporating sustainability criteria into procurement decisions, organizations can drive positive change throughout their supplier networks and reduce the overall environmental and social footprint of their supply chains.

sources.title: Supply Management Procurement
Procurement vs Supply Chain Management: What's the Difference?
SCM Definitions and Glossary of Terms

source_concept.label: Describe sales and operations planning

relationships.correct_description: Demand forecasting is a critical input to the S&OP demand planning step
relationships.explanation: S&OP includes demand forecasting as a critical input because the demand planning step, which is the second of the five main S&OP stages, relies on forecasts to establish the expected sales volumes that drive all subsequent planning decisions. The accuracy of these demand forecasts directly affects the quality of the production plans, inventory targets, and capacity decisions that S&OP produces. This inclusion relationship reflects the hierarchical nature of S&OP as an integrative process that consumes and synthesizes outputs from multiple subordinate planning functions.

sources.title: Sales and operations planning
What Is Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)?
Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)

source_concept.label: Describe sales and operations planning

target_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Inventory planning is part of the supply planning phase of S&OP
relationships.explanation: S&OP includes inventory planning within its supply planning phase because determining appropriate inventory levels is essential to balancing supply capacity with demand requirements. During the supply planning stage of S&OP, inventory targets are set based on the demand plan, production capabilities, and service level objectives. This inclusion ensures that inventory decisions are made in the context of the broader organizational plan rather than as isolated operational decisions, enabling better alignment between customer expectations and supply chain capabilities.

sources.title: Sales and operations planning
What Is Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)?
Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)

source_concept.label: Describe sales and operations planning

relationships.correct_description: CRM demand signals and customer insights feed into S&OP demand planning
relationships.explanation: S&OP is related to CRM because the demand planning component of S&OP benefits from customer intelligence captured in CRM systems, including sales pipeline data, customer commitments, and market feedback. CRM provides the granular, customer-level demand signals that complement statistical forecasts in the S&OP demand planning step. This connection ensures that the operational plans developed through S&OP are grounded in real customer relationship data rather than relying solely on historical trend extrapolation.

sources.title: Sales and operations planning
What Is Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)?
Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)

source_concept.label: Define supply market intelligence

relationships.correct_description: Supply market intelligence informs procurement strategies and supplier selection
relationships.explanation: Supply market intelligence is a prerequisite for effective procurement because strategic sourcing decisions require comprehensive knowledge of the supplier landscape, market pricing dynamics, and supply risk factors. Without market intelligence, procurement professionals lack the information needed to identify the best suppliers, negotiate competitive terms, or anticipate supply disruptions. This prerequisite relationship reflects the principle that informed procurement decisions, grounded in thorough market analysis, consistently outperform reactive purchasing approaches that lack market context.

sources.title: Why Your Procurement Team Needs Supply Market Intelligence
Supply market intelligence: definition, methods and benefits in Procurement
Supply Chain Intelligence Definition

source_concept.label: Define supply market intelligence

relationships.correct_description: Analytics capabilities enable the data processing behind supply market intelligence
relationships.explanation: Supply market intelligence is related to analytics because the systematic gathering and interpretation of market data that defines supply market intelligence fundamentally relies on analytical tools and methodologies. Analytics provides the computational capabilities to process large datasets of supplier information, pricing trends, and market dynamics that form the basis of actionable market intelligence. This relationship demonstrates how analytics serves as an enabling technology for supply market intelligence, turning raw market data into strategic procurement insights.

sources.title: Why Your Procurement Team Needs Supply Market Intelligence
Supply market intelligence: definition, methods and benefits in Procurement
Supply Chain Intelligence Definition

source_concept.label: Define supply market intelligence

relationships.correct_description: Market intelligence data supports supply-side forecasting and trend analysis
relationships.explanation: Supply market intelligence contributes to forecasting by providing supply-side data that complements demand-side forecasting models. Market intelligence on raw material availability, supplier capacity changes, and geopolitical factors helps organizations forecast potential supply constraints and price fluctuations. This relationship shows how forecasting benefits from both demand signals and supply market conditions, enabling more comprehensive planning that accounts for constraints on both sides of the supply-demand equation.

sources.title: Why Your Procurement Team Needs Supply Market Intelligence
Supply market intelligence: definition, methods and benefits in Procurement
Supply Chain Intelligence Definition

source_concept.label: Define sustainability for supply chain management

relationships.correct_description: Sustainable procurement practices evaluate suppliers on environmental and social criteria
relationships.explanation: Sustainability applies to procurement by introducing environmental and social evaluation criteria into the supplier selection and purchasing process. Sustainable procurement extends traditional procurement criteria beyond cost, quality, and delivery to include factors such as carbon footprint, labor practices, waste management, and ethical sourcing. This application transforms procurement from a purely economic function into a strategic lever for achieving broader organizational sustainability goals, as supplier choices have cascading effects throughout the supply chain.

sources.title: What is Sustainable Supply Chain Management? (SSCM)

What Is Supply Chain Sustainability and Why Is It Important?

source_concept.label: Define sustainability for supply chain management

target_concept.label: Explain the relationship between supply chain management and transportation planning

relationships.correct_description: Transportation planning must consider emissions reduction and green logistics
relationships.explanation: Sustainability relates to transportation planning because transportation is one of the largest contributors to supply chain carbon emissions, making it a primary target for green logistics initiatives. Sustainability requirements push transportation planners to consider fuel-efficient routing, mode shifting from air to sea or rail, vehicle fleet electrification, and shipment consolidation to reduce emissions. This connection highlights how environmental sustainability goals directly influence transportation strategy and operational decisions, requiring planners to balance cost efficiency with environmental impact reduction.

sources.title: What is Sustainable Supply Chain Management? (SSCM)
State of Supply Chain Sustainability
What Is Supply Chain Sustainability and Why Is It Important?

source_concept.label: Define sustainability for supply chain management

target_concept.label: Explain the growth of global distribution

relationships.correct_description: Global distribution networks face increasing pressure to reduce their environmental footprint
relationships.explanation: Sustainability is related to global distribution because the expansion of international supply networks has amplified environmental concerns including carbon emissions from long-distance freight, packaging waste from complex logistics chains, and resource consumption across multiple distribution points. As stakeholders and regulators demand greater environmental accountability, global distribution strategies must increasingly incorporate sustainability considerations such as nearshoring to reduce transportation distances, optimizing distribution network design to minimize emissions, and implementing circular economy practices across international operations.

sources.title: What is Sustainable Supply Chain Management? (SSCM)
State of Supply Chain Sustainability
What Is Supply Chain Sustainability and Why Is It Important?

source_concept.label: Explain the relationship between supply chain management and transportation planning

target_concept.label: Explain the growth of global distribution

relationships.correct_description: Global distribution relies on international transportation planning across multiple modes
relationships.explanation: Transportation planning is closely related to global distribution because the physical movement of goods across international borders requires coordinating multiple transportation modes including ocean freight, air cargo, rail, and trucking. Effective transportation planning enables global distribution by optimizing carrier selection, routing, and mode combinations to balance cost, speed, and reliability across international supply chains. As global distribution networks expand, transportation planning complexity increases with factors such as customs clearance, intermodal transfers, and cross-border regulatory compliance.

sources.title: Managing Transportation and Logistics in Supply Chains
The Role of Transportation in Supply Chain Management
What is Transportation Planning in logistics?

source_concept.label: Explain the relationship between supply chain management and transportation planning

target_concept.label: Define sustainability for supply chain management

relationships.correct_description: Transportation planning must address sustainability through emissions reduction and green logistics
relationships.explanation: Transportation planning is related to sustainability because transportation activities account for a significant portion of supply chain carbon emissions and environmental impact. Transportation planners must increasingly incorporate sustainability objectives into their decisions, including selecting lower-emission modes, optimizing routes to reduce fuel consumption, and consolidating shipments to minimize the number of trips. This relationship reflects the growing expectation that transportation planning serves not only cost and service goals but also environmental responsibility through measurable emissions reduction.

sources.title: Managing Transportation and Logistics in Supply Chains
The Role of Transportation in Supply Chain Management
What is Transportation Planning in logistics?

source_concept.label: Explain the relationship between supply chain management and transportation planning

target_concept.label: Explain the logistics of inventory management

relationships.correct_description: Transportation planning coordinates with inventory management for timely replenishment
relationships.explanation: Transportation planning and inventory management are closely related because the timing and reliability of transportation directly affects inventory availability and replenishment cycles. Transportation lead times are a key variable in inventory calculations for reorder points and safety stock levels, while transportation costs influence economic order quantities and shipment frequency decisions. Effective coordination between transportation planning and inventory management ensures that replenishment shipments arrive on time to prevent stockouts while avoiding the costs of expedited shipping or excessive safety stock buffers.

sources.title: Managing Transportation and Logistics in Supply Chains
The Role of Transportation in Supply Chain Management
What is Transportation Planning in logistics?

source_concept.label: Describe careers associated with supply chain management

relationships.correct_description: Analytics skills are increasingly important for supply chain career advancement
relationships.explanation: Careers in supply chain management are increasingly related to analytics because the growing adoption of data-driven decision-making across procurement, logistics, and operations has made analytical skills a core competency for SCM professionals. Supply chain analyst roles specifically require proficiency in data analysis, forecasting tools, and business intelligence platforms. As organizations invest in digital supply chain transformation, professionals who can leverage analytics to optimize operations, identify inefficiencies, and support strategic decisions command significant career advantages.

sources.title: Careers in Supply Chain Management
Supply Chain Management Careers
Starting Your SCM Career

source_concept.label: Describe careers associated with supply chain management

relationships.correct_description: Procurement is one of the major career specialization paths in SCM
relationships.explanation: Careers in supply chain management are related to procurement because procurement specialist and strategic sourcing roles represent one of the most well-established career tracks within SCM. Procurement professionals manage supplier relationships, negotiate contracts, and make purchasing decisions that directly impact organizational costs and supply reliability. This career path offers progression from buyer and purchasing agent roles through to chief procurement officer positions, reflecting procurement's importance as both a functional discipline and a strategic business function.

sources.title: Careers in Supply Chain Management
Supply Chain Management Careers
Starting Your SCM Career

source_concept.label: Describe careers associated with supply chain management

target_concept.label: Explain the relationship between supply chain management and transportation planning

relationships.correct_description: Transportation and logistics management represents a major SCM career track
relationships.explanation: Careers in supply chain management are related to transportation planning because logistics and transportation management constitutes one of the largest and most visible career tracks in SCM. Roles such as transportation manager, logistics coordinator, and fleet operations manager are among the most common SCM positions. Given that transportation can account for up to 60 percent of total operational costs, professionals who can optimize transportation networks, negotiate carrier contracts, and leverage transportation management systems are highly valued in the industry.

sources.title: Careers in Supply Chain Management
Supply Chain Management Careers
